name: Georgia
tagline: A $38.5B FY2027 budget, a fast-maturing statewide AI governance program, and one of the nation's hottest data-center markets now facing a tax and energy reckoning — Georgia is reshaping how it adopts and secures technology.

execSummary.summary: Georgia budgets annually on a July–June fiscal year. Governor Kemp signed the $38.5B FY2027 budget in May 2026, a fiscally cautious plan that directs agencies to withhold more than $300M in proposed new spending — a 'disciplined growth' signal sellers should price in. Three structural themes matter for SLG sellers. First, the Georgia Technology Authority (GTA) runs a centralized, security-first technology motion under State CIO Shawnzia Thomas, whose 2026 philosophy is explicitly 'AI Governance Is Security' — NIST controls applied to AI the same as any other system, with every agency required to publish an AI usage plan by Dec 31, 2026. Second, Georgia is deploying ~$1.3B in BEAD broadband funding through GTA, moving from planning into build-out. Third, metro Atlanta is one of the country's largest data-center hubs; a 2026 legislative push to suspend tax exemptions and shift energy costs (SB 410, SB 34, HB 1063) failed when the session ended April 3, leaving Georgia's data-center tax breaks intact — though energy/grid scrutiny and local moratoriums persist. For Dell, the buying motion runs through GTA Direct and statewide contracts, and the security-first culture rewards governed, on-prem and private-cloud AI for sensitive workloads.
opportunities: Governed AI adoption as agencies build mandated AI usage plans (due Dec 31, 2026) — lead with private/on-prem AI and NIST-aligned controls for sensitive data.
Cybersecurity modernization tied to GTA's 'security is security' posture and statewide exercises like Cyber Dawg.
AI-infrastructure and ISG demand from the metro-Atlanta data-center market and the University System of Georgia's research computing.
Endpoint, client, and lifecycle refresh across a large, centralized state enterprise under disciplined-growth budgeting.

stats.label: BEAD Broadband
stats.note: Federal funds via GTA; final proposal approved Nov 2025

budget.intro: Georgia budgets on an annual July–June fiscal cycle, with an Amended FY (AFY) mid-year adjustment. The General Assembly passed and Governor Kemp signed a $38.5B budget for FY2027 in May 2026 — about 2% (~$738M) above current spending. There is no single 'state IT budget' line; technology spend is distributed across agencies, with GTA providing centralized infrastructure, security, and procurement services and named programs (broadband, AI) funded separately.
notes: Georgia's fiscal year runs July 1–June 30; figures here are annual unless noted.
FY2027 is a disciplined-growth budget — >$300M of proposed new spending was held back, so confirm an agency's funded scope before forecasting.
BEAD is multi-year federal money administered by GTA, not a single-year state appropriation.

FY2027 (governor's proposed, per NASBO): $32.9B general-fund appropriations and $38.5B state funds; $76.5B total funds. Fiscally cautious — the Revenue Shortfall Reserve sits at $5.6B ('filled') alongside a fourth $1.0B tax rebate and a rate cut to 4.99%.

initiatives.name: Statewide AI Governance Framework
initiatives.summary: GTA's Office of Artificial Intelligence runs a maturing framework: an AI Roadmap and Governance Framework, a Generative AI Responsible Use Standard, a Georgia Board for Artificial Intelligence, and a mandate that every agency publish a detailed AI usage plan by Dec 31, 2026. Governance is treated as security (NIST controls applied to AI). This is an opening for governed, auditable, private/on-prem AI on sensitive data rather than open public models.

initiatives.name: 'AI Governance Is Security' — Cyber Posture
initiatives.summary: Under State CIO Shawnzia Thomas, Georgia's 2026 philosophy applies the same NIST controls — access control, audit logging, data protection, oversight — to AI as to any other technology. The state runs large-scale exercises (Cyber Dawg) and a July 2025 Innovation Lab that uses synthetic data only. Security-first culture rewards vendors who lead with controls, not just capability.

initiatives.name: BEAD Broadband Build-Out
initiatives.summary: GTA administers ~$1.3B in federal BEAD funding to close Georgia's digital divide. After an NTIA-mandated restructuring in mid-2025, Georgia's updated final proposal was approved Nov 18, 2025, and the program is moving from planning into implementation. Leftover funds can support workforce, telehealth, cybersecurity, and digital-literacy use cases.

initiatives.name: Metro-Atlanta Data-Center Market
initiatives.summary: Metro Atlanta is a top-tier U.S. data-center hub (Microsoft, QTS, CoreSite, DataBank and others). The 2026 session (ended April 3) saw a flood of bills targeting data-center tax exemptions and energy costs (SB 410, SB 34, HB 1063), but none cleared both chambers — Georgia left its data-center sales-tax breaks intact despite an estimated $2.5B in 2026 forgone revenue. The policy cloud has lifted for now, keeping this the strongest Dell AI-infrastructure tailwind in the state, though energy/grid scrutiny and local moratoriums persist.

procurement.intro: The Georgia Technology Authority (GTA) is the state's central IT and cybersecurity agency. Most state technology buys run through GTA Direct — a managed-services path that gives agencies, local governments, colleges/universities, and boards of education access to IT services through pre-qualified, market-leading providers. For transactions over $25,000, GTA procurement uses solicitation types (RFQ, RFP, RFQC) prioritized by 'best value,' often establishing pools of pre-qualified contractors. GTA also endorses major agency technology procurements.

vehicles.name: GTA Direct
vehicles.detail: Primary managed-services path. Pre-qualified providers; open to state agencies, local gov, higher-ed, and boards of education. Confirm Dell coverage and resellers are current.

vehicles.name: GTA-endorsed agency procurements
vehicles.detail: Large agency technology buys require GTA endorsement — engage GTA early to shape requirements.

vehicles.name: RFQC pre-qualified pools
vehicles.detail: Request for Qualified Contractors establishes vendor pools; getting into the pool is the gate to compete.

vehicles.name: AI governance review
vehicles.detail: Not a contract, but AI/software purchases flow through GTA's AI governance and software-approval process — clear it early.

entities.name: GTA
entities.role: Central IT, cybersecurity, AI governance, broadband, and statewide procurement — leads the technology motion.

entities.name: Dept. of Administrative Services (DOAS)
entities.role: Statewide non-IT procurement authority; coordinates with GTA on enterprise buys.

entities.name: University System of Georgia (USG)
entities.role: 26 institutions; large, semi-independent research-computing and AI-infrastructure buyer.

entities.name: Dept. of Community Health / large agencies
entities.role: Data-heavy modernization and among the larger agency technology spenders.

procurement.competition: Georgia is a centralized, governance-driven market — GTA's pre-qualified pools and 'best value' model mean incumbents hold position across compute, security, and services. Differentiation comes from NIST-aligned, governed-AI-ready offerings, security-first packaging that fits the 'AI Governance Is Security' culture, and AI-infrastructure capability mapped to the data-center and USG research buyers — not price alone.

powerStructure.name: Brian Kemp
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Signed the $38.5B FY2027 budget; fiscally disciplined, withheld >$300M in new spending.

powerStructure.name: Shawnzia Thomas
powerStructure.title: State CIO & GTA Executive Director
powerStructure.note: Owns statewide tech direction; 2026 philosophy 'AI Governance Is Security.'

powerStructure.name: GTA Office of Artificial Intelligence
powerStructure.title: AI governance & standards
powerStructure.note: Runs the AI roadmap, GenAI Responsible Use Standard, and agency AI-plan mandate (due Dec 31, 2026).

powerStructure.name: Georgia General Assembly
powerStructure.title: Appropriations & legislation
powerStructure.note: Sets the annual budget; in the 2026 session (ended April 3) several data-center tax/energy bills failed, leaving exemptions intact.

powerStructure.name: University System of Georgia
powerStructure.title: Higher-ed system
powerStructure.note: 26 institutions; large independent research-computing and AI-infrastructure buyer.

news.title: Kemp signs $38.5B FY2027 budget; directs >$300M in withheld new spending
news.impact: Disciplined-growth budget — confirm funded scope before forecasting agency deals.

news.title: Georgia 2026 session ends; data-center tax/energy bills fail, exemptions intact
news.impact: Policy cloud lifts for new data-center builds — keeps metro Atlanta a strong Dell AI-infrastructure tailwind, though energy/grid scrutiny persists.

news.title: GTA releases FY2025 Annual State IT Report
news.impact: Highlights agencywide AI licensing/training and secured broadband funding — signals where modernization dollars flow.

news.title: Gov. Kemp proposed a $32.9B general-fund FY2027 budget
news.impact: $76.5B total funds ($38.5B state funds); fiscally cautious with the Revenue Shortfall Reserve 'filled' at $5.6B, a fourth $1.0B tax rebate, and an income-tax rate cut to 4.99%.

news.title: Georgia's 2026 tech philosophy framed as 'AI Governance Is Security'
news.impact: Security-first buying culture; lead with NIST-aligned, governed AI.

news.title: NTIA approves Georgia's updated BEAD final proposal (~$1.3B)
news.impact: Broadband program moves from planning into implementation via GTA.

dellActions: Lead with governed, private/on-prem AI as agencies build mandated AI usage plans (due Dec 31, 2026) — map offerings to GTA's NIST-aligned governance framework.
Package security-first to fit the 'AI Governance Is Security' culture — emphasize access control, audit logging, and data protection, not just capability.
Validate Dell's GTA Direct coverage and reseller scope, and get into the relevant RFQC pre-qualified pools before pursuing agency deals.
Tie Dell ISG / AI-infrastructure offerings to the metro-Atlanta data-center market and University System of Georgia research computing — while tracking the 2026 tax/energy policy outcome.
Engage GTA early on endorsed agency procurements and the Innovation Lab to shape requirements ahead of competition.
